summary: Tune the operational Lambda functions at the configuration level Terraform safely owns: memory, timeout, reserved and provisioned concurrency, environment variables, and publish behavior, plus a guardrailed full delete. Function code deploys out-of-band, so the deployable-code lifecycle is deliberately out of scope. Memory, timeout, and publish changes apply live with no downtime and are the most L1-safe surface; concurrency and environment changes carry shared-pool or application risk and take two approvals; deleting a function breaks every caller instantly, so it stays engineer-only. Environment-variable edits push toward secret references, never plaintext values.

operations.title: Resize function memory
operations.summary: Resize a function's memory, which also scales its CPU and network share. Takes effect on the next run, with no downtime.
operations.description: Change the memory allocated to a function, which also scales its proportional CPU and network bandwidth. Applies to the next invocation with no downtime; in-flight executions finish on the old allocation.

params.name: function
params.label: Function
params.help: Lambda function to resize.

params.name: new_memory_size
params.label: New memory (MB)

bounds.semantic: AWS accepts any integer 128-10240 MB; CPU/network scale proportionally

params.help: Higher memory reduces duration for CPU-bound work but raises per-ms cost; too low risks out-of-memory kills.

operations.title: Adjust function timeout
operations.summary: Change how long a function is allowed to run before AWS cuts it off. Takes effect on the next run, with no downtime.
operations.description: Change how long AWS lets an invocation run before it is forcibly killed. Takes effect on the next invocation, no downtime.

params.name: function
params.label: Function
params.help: Lambda function whose timeout to change.

params.name: new_timeout
params.label: New timeout (seconds)

bounds.semantic: 900s (15 min) is the hard AWS ceiling

params.help: Too short truncates legitimate long-running work; too long lets a hung dependency hold a concurrency slot for the full duration.

operations.title: Set reserved concurrent executions
operations.summary: Reserve part of the account's shared invocation capacity for this function; too much starves other functions.
operations.description: Carve out a fixed slice of the account's shared Lambda concurrency pool for this function, which also caps its own maximum concurrency. Too low throttles the function under load; too high starves concurrency headroom from every other function in the account.

params.name: function
params.label: Function
params.help: Lambda function to set reserved concurrency on.

params.name: new_reserved_concurrent_executions
params.label: New reserved concurrent executions

bounds.semantic: carved out of the account-wide unreserved pool (per-region account default 1000); 0 fully throttles the function

params.help: Set to -1 (via the delete-reservation op, not here) to go back to unreserved. 0 blocks all invocations — use deliberately as an emergency stop.

operations.title: Add provisioned concurrency
operations.summary: Keep a set number of instances pre-warmed for a published version to remove cold starts; billed continuously.
operations.description: Create an aws_lambda_provisioned_concurrency_config that pre-initializes a fixed number of execution environments for a published function version or alias, eliminating cold starts for that qualifier. Bills continuously for the reserved capacity whether invoked or not, and requires a published version (not $LATEST) to attach to.

params.name: function
params.label: Function
params.help: Lambda function to add provisioned concurrency to.

params.name: qualifier
params.label: Version or alias

bounds.semantic: must be a published version number or an alias name; $LATEST is rejected by AWS

params.help: The function must already have a published version to pin this to.

params.name: provisioned_concurrent_executions
params.label: Provisioned concurrency units

bounds.semantic: each unit is billed continuously regardless of invocation volume

params.help: Size to expected steady-state concurrent invocations; over-provisioning is a direct, ongoing cost.

operations.title: Add an environment variable
operations.summary: Add a new environment variable to a function; use a secret-store reference for anything sensitive.
operations.description: Add a new key to the function's environment variable map. Takes effect immediately on the next invocation; a bad value can break the function on its first cold start after apply. Changing a key that already holds a different value, or a function whose Terraform declares no environment block yet, is refused and routed to an engineer. Never put a raw credential in the value — point at a Secrets Manager ARN or SSM parameter name and have the function fetch the real secret at runtime.

params.name: function
params.label: Function
params.help: Lambda function whose environment to change.

params.name: env_key
params.label: Variable name
params.help: New key to add. A key the function already sets to a different value is refused (routed to an engineer) rather than silently overwritten.

params.name: env_value
params.label: Value (or secret reference)

bounds.semantic: non-secret config value, or a Secrets Manager/SSM Parameter Store name/ARN — never a raw credential

params.help: Plaintext config values only. For anything sensitive, store the actual secret in Secrets Manager/SSM and put its ARN or parameter name here instead.

operations.title: Toggle version publishing on deploy
operations.summary: Turn automatic version publishing on or off for future code deployments. No effect until code is next deployed.
operations.description: Enable or disable automatic publishing of an immutable version each time the function's code changes — required before pinning aliases or provisioned concurrency to a specific version. In this estate function code is deliberately managed out-of-band (filename/source_code_hash are in lifecycle.ignore_changes), so flipping this alone publishes nothing until code is next deployed in-band.

params.name: function
params.label: Function
params.help: Lambda function to change publish behaviour on.

params.name: publish
params.label: Publish new version on code change
params.help: true creates a new immutable version on every future in-band code deploy; false leaves only $LATEST mutable.

operations.title: Delete a Lambda function
operations.summary: Permanently delete a function; every caller starts failing immediately. This can't be undone, so an engineer reviews it first.
operations.description: Permanently remove the function from AWS and from Terraform state. Anything invoking it — API Gateway routes, EventBridge rules, S3 notifications, Step Functions, other Lambdas — starts failing immediately with ResourceNotFoundException. There is no soft-delete or undo once applied; the function, its versions, and its aliases are gone.

params.name: function
params.label: Function
params.help: Lambda function to delete. Confirm no live triggers/callers depend on it first.

operations.title: Set function instruction set architecture
operations.summary: Switch a function between x86 and ARM processors. The deployed code must already support the one you pick, or calls fail.
operations.description: Set the function's `architectures` value to x86_64 or arm64. The already-deployed code package must support the chosen instruction set; a mismatch causes invocation failures. Applies to the next invocation with no platform downtime; reversible by switching back.

params.name: function
params.label: Function
params.help: Lambda function whose architecture to set.

params.name: architecture
params.label: Instruction set architecture

bounds.semantic: Doc: architectures valid values are ["x86_64"] or ["arm64"], default ["x86_64"]. The deployment package must support the chosen architecture.

params.help: arm64 (Graviton) is typically cheaper per-ms but the deployed package must be built for it.

operations.title: Set application log level
operations.summary: Set how detailed a function's own application logs are. Only takes effect when logs are in structured JSON format.
operations.description: Set `logging_config.application_log_level`, the detail level of your function's own application logs sent to CloudWatch Logs. Only takes effect when the function's log format is JSON (structured logging). In-place, applies to the next invocation, reversible.

params.name: function
params.label: Function
params.help: Lambda function whose application log level to set.

params.name: application_log_level
params.label: Application log level

bounds.semantic: Doc: logging_config.application_log_level valid values are TRACE, DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERROR, FATAL. Effective only when log_format is JSON.

params.help: More verbose levels (TRACE/DEBUG) raise log volume and CloudWatch cost.

operations.title: Set system log level
operations.summary: Set how detailed Lambda's own platform logs are for a function. Only takes effect when logs are in JSON format.
operations.description: Set `logging_config.system_log_level`, the detail level of Lambda platform (system) logs sent to CloudWatch Logs. Only takes effect when the function's log format is JSON (structured logging). In-place, applies to the next invocation, reversible.

params.name: function
params.label: Function
params.help: Lambda function whose system log level to set.

params.name: system_log_level
params.label: System log level

bounds.semantic: Doc: logging_config.system_log_level valid values are DEBUG, INFO, WARN. Effective only when log_format is JSON.

params.help: DEBUG emits verbose Lambda platform logs; INFO/WARN are quieter.

operations.title: Set the publish target
operations.summary: Set where a new version lands when publishing is turned on. Has no effect unless publishing is enabled.
operations.description: Set the `publish_to` argument, which selects where a published version/alias is created when `publish` is enabled. The provider accepts only the value LATEST_PUBLISHED; omit it for regular version publishing. In-place, reversible.

params.name: function
params.label: Function
params.help: Lambda function whose publish_to behavior to set.

params.name: publish_to
params.label: Publish target

bounds.semantic: Doc: publish_to's only accepted value is LATEST_PUBLISHED.

params.help: Only takes effect alongside publish=true; in this estate code is managed out-of-band so nothing publishes until code is next deployed in-band.

operations.title: Enable SnapStart
operations.summary: Turn on SnapStart to cut cold-start time for published versions. The code must tolerate being paused and resumed from a snapshot.
operations.description: Set the `snap_start` block's `apply_on` to PublishedVersions, enabling SnapStart so Lambda pre-initializes and caches a snapshot of published versions to reduce cold-start latency. Requires the deployed code to be safe under snapshot-and-restore semantics (no assumptions about unique in-memory state, connections, or randomness fixed at init). Applies to newly published versions; reversible by removing the block.

params.name: function
params.label: Function
params.help: Lambda function to enable SnapStart on.

params.name: apply_on
params.label: Apply SnapStart on

bounds.semantic: Doc: snap_start.apply_on's only accepted value is PublishedVersions. There is no disable value — remove the block to turn SnapStart off.

params.help: SnapStart optimizes published versions; the function must publish versions and its code must tolerate snapshot restore.

operations.title: Toggle retaining old Lambda layer versions
operations.summary: Keep the previous Lambda layer version instead of deleting it when this function is destroyed. No effect right now.
operations.description: Set the function-level `skip_destroy` flag, which controls whether the old version of a previously deployed Lambda Layer is retained rather than deleted when the resource is destroyed. Default is false. In-place metadata change with no effect on the live running function's current state; reversible.

params.name: function
params.label: Function
params.help: Lambda function whose skip_destroy flag to set.

params.name: skip_destroy
params.label: Retain old Lambda Layer version on destroy

bounds.semantic: Doc: skip_destroy is a boolean, default false.

params.help: true retains the previously deployed Lambda Layer version instead of deleting it on destroy.

operations.title: Set eventual-consistency propagation timeout behavior
operations.summary: Applying this REPLACES the function: new identity and a brief outage while it's recreated. An engineer reviews it first.
operations.description: Set `use_resource_timeout_for_propagation`, which controls whether the provider applies resource-level timeout values while retrying eventually-consistent API operations instead of its default 5-minute propagation timeout. Applying it forces replacement of the function: it is destroyed and recreated with a new identity and a brief outage, so an engineer authors and reviews it. Default is false.

params.name: function
params.label: Function
params.help: Lambda function whose propagation-timeout behavior to set.

params.name: use_resource_timeout_for_propagation
params.label: Use resource-level timeout for propagation

bounds.semantic: Doc: use_resource_timeout_for_propagation is a boolean, default false (provider uses a 5-minute propagation timeout by default).

params.help: true makes the provider honor resource-level timeouts while waiting on eventually-consistent operations instead of the default 5-minute window.

operations.title: Create a new function
operations.summary: Request a new serverless function; an engineer finalizes and reviews the Terraform before it's applied.
operations.description: Specify a small serverless function — runtime, sizing and network placement. The code package, its execution role and its triggers are always delivered by the engineer, never uploaded here. Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish.

params.name: function_name
params.label: Function name
params.help: Letters, numbers, dashes and underscores.

params.name: runtime
params.label: Runtime
params.help: The language the function runs on. Pick engineer-decides if unsure.

params.name: handler
params.label: Handler
params.help: The entry point inside the code package, like app.handler. Leave blank for the engineer.

params.name: memory_mb
params.label: Memory (MB)
params.help: Memory also scales the processor share.

params.name: timeout_seconds
params.label: Timeout (seconds)
params.help: How long one invocation may run before it is cut off.

params.name: inside_vpc
params.label: Runs inside the network (VPC)?
params.help: Yes only when the function must reach private resources like the database.

params.name: subnets
params.label: Subnets
params.help: Private subnets the function attaches to.

params.name: security_groups
params.label: Security groups
params.help: Who the function may talk to inside the network.

params.name: resource_name
params.label: Function name tag
params.help: Shown as the name everywhere — consoles, tickets, this portal.

params.name: description_tag
params.label: What this function does
params.help: One line an operator can act on at 3 AM.

params.name: pic
params.label: Person in charge
params.help: Who to call about this function.

decisions: Code package (S3 or zip) — delivered by the engineer, never uploaded here
The execution role and its permissions
Trigger wiring — schedule, notification topic, or load balancer
Environment variables are deliberately excluded here — they can carry secrets

operations.title: Add a function URL
operations.summary: Request a dedicated HTTPS endpoint that invokes a function directly. An engineer finalizes and reviews the Terraform before it's applied.
operations.description: Specify a new aws_lambda_function_url for an existing function: its authorization mode and invoke mode. This is a public invocation endpoint on the internet the moment it exists — authorization_type controls who can call it, not whether it's reachable. Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish.

params.name: url_label
params.label: Function URL label
params.help: Lower-case letters, numbers and dashes. Names this URL config in the generated draft — a function URL has no name of its own.

params.name: function
params.label: Function
params.help: The function this URL invokes.

params.name: authorization_type
params.label: Authorization
params.help: AWS_IAM requires every caller to sign requests with SigV4 credentials. NONE makes the URL callable by anyone on the internet with no authentication at all.

params.name: invoke_mode
params.label: Invoke mode
params.help: RESPONSE_STREAM streams the response as the function generates it, instead of buffering the whole payload before returning. The function code must be written to stream.

keywords: public http endpoint for a function
decisions: authorization_type NONE makes this URL callable by anyone on the internet with no authentication — confirm that is genuinely intended before approving; AWS_IAM (the default here) is the safe starting point
CORS configuration (the cors block: allowed origins/methods/headers) is an engineer follow-up if browser clients call this URL directly
Pinning to a specific published version or alias (qualifier) instead of $LATEST is an engineer decision

operations.title: Add an event source mapping
operations.summary: Request a new poll-based trigger that invokes a function per batch. An engineer finalizes and reviews the Terraform before it's applied.
operations.description: Specify a new aws_lambda_event_source_mapping that polls an SQS queue and invokes a function with each batch. DynamoDB Streams, Kinesis, MSK, and other poll-based sources are engineer follow-ups outside this form's SQS-queue common case. Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish.

params.name: mapping_label
params.label: Mapping label
params.help: Lower-case letters, numbers and dashes. Names this mapping in the generated draft — AWS assigns it its own UUID; this label is only for the request.

params.name: function
params.label: Function
params.help: The function this mapping invokes for each batch.

params.name: event_source
params.label: Event source (SQS queue)
params.help: The SQS queue that triggers this function. DynamoDB Streams, Kinesis, MSK, and other poll-based sources are an engineer follow-up — this form covers the common SQS case.

params.name: batch_size
params.label: Batch size
params.help: Maximum number of messages Lambda reads from the queue before invoking the function once with the batch.

params.name: enabled
params.label: Enabled
params.help: Off creates the mapping in a disabled (paused) state — nothing polls until it's turned on.

params.name: function_response_types
params.label: Partial batch failure reporting
params.help: ReportBatchItemFailures lets the function report which individual messages in a batch failed, instead of the whole batch being retried.

params.name: resource_name
params.label: Mapping name tag
params.help: Shown as the name everywhere — consoles, tickets, this portal.

params.name: description_tag
params.label: What this trigger is for
params.help: One line an operator can act on at 3 AM.

params.name: pic
params.label: Person in charge
params.help: Who to call about this trigger.

decisions: DynamoDB Streams, Kinesis, MSK/self-managed Kafka, and Amazon MQ sources are engineer follow-ups — this form covers the common SQS-queue case
A destination for failed records (destination_config.on_failure), filter criteria, and a non-default starting position are engineer decisions
